stem: A hospital uses a CT scan screening to screen for early-stage lung cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 85% and a specificity of 97%. The prevalence of early-stage lung cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 0.6% and 1.4%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has early-stage lung cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(early-stage lung cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.146 to 0.287 as prevalence varies from 0.6% to 1.4%. Point estimate at 1.0% prevalence: 0.223.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.03.
The confidence interval [0.146, 0.287] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (85%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
